en.extract:
The crested caracara (Caracara plancus) is a bird of prey (raptor) in the falcon family, Falconidae. It was formerly placed in the genus Polyborus before being given in its own genus, Caracara. It is native to and found in the southern and southeastern United States, Mexico (where it is present in every state) and the majority of mainland Latin America, as well as some Caribbean islands. The crested caracara is quite adaptable and hardy, for a species found predominantly in the neotropics; it can be found in a range of environments and ecosystems, including semi-arid and desert climates, maritime or coastal areas, subtropical and tropical forests, temperate regions, plains, swamps, and even in urban areas. Documented, albeit rare, sightings have occurred as far north as Minnesota and the Canadian provinces of Alberta, Ontario, and Prince Edward Island. The southern extent of the crested caracara's distribution can reach as far as Tierra del Fuego and Magallanes Region, Chile.


Etymology
The word "caracara" ultimately traces its origins to Old Tupi karakará. Further etymology is uncertain, but it is assumed to be onomatopoeic, imitating the bird's call.
The crested caracara is also known as the Mexican eagle, although it is not a true eagle. This stems from a 1960 proposal by Rafael Martín del Campo, supposedly based on an analysis of pre-Conquest imagery, that the "bird atop a prickly pear" associated with the mythical founding of Tenochtitlan was a caracara, and not a golden eagle (which is a rare sight in Mexico, especially as far south as Tenochtitlan) as is traditionally depicted. Nonetheless, since at least the Spanish Conquest, the golden eagle has been the bird irrevocably associated with the founding myth, featuring prominently in independent Mexico's national insignia as well as being its national animal.


"Texan eagle"
Balduin Möllhausen, the German artist accompanying the 1853 railroad survey (led by Lt. Amiel Weeks Whipple) from the Canadian River to California along the 35th parallel, recounted observing what he called the "Texan Eagle", which, in his account, he identified as Audubon's Polyborus vulgaris. This sighting occurred in the Sans Bois Mountains in southeastern Oklahoma.


Taxonomy
In 1777, English illustrator John Frederick Miller included a hand-coloured plate of the crested caracara in his Icones animalium et plantarum ("icons of the animal and plant world"). He coined the binomial name Falco plancus and specified the type locality as Tierra del Fuego. The specific epithet plancus is Latin for "eagle". The crested caracara is now placed in the genus Caracara (which was introduced in 1826 by German naturalist Blasius Merrem).
Two subspecies are recognised:

C. p. cheriway (Jacquin, 1784) – United States (Southern California, Arizona, Florida, Louisiana, New Mexico, Texas), México (present in every state), Belize, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Panamá, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, and north Roraima, Brazil; Caribbean islands of Cuba, Aruba, Guanaja and Roatán (Honduras), and Trinidad; the Pacific Islas Marías (Mexico) and Isla del Rey (Panamá). Individual birds have been seen as far north as Dallas, Texas and Santa Cruz, California.
C. p. plancus (Miller, JF, 1777) – SE Perú, N Bolivia to Eastern Brazil, south to Tierra del Fuego and the Falkland Islands.
The subspecies C. p. cheriway was formerly classed as a separate species, with the common English name of the northern crested caracara.


Distribution and habitat

The crested caracara occurs from Tierra del Fuego in southernmost South America to the southern United States, Mexico, and Central America. An isolated population occurs on the Falkland Islands. It avoids the Andean highlands and dense humid forests, such as the Amazon rainforest, where it is largely restricted to relatively open sections along major rivers. Otherwise, it occurs in virtually any open or semi-open habitat and is often found near humans.
Reports have been made of the crested caracara as far north as San Francisco, California. and, in 2012, near Crescent City, California. Some are believed to possibly be living in Nova Scotia, with numerous sightings throughout the 2010s. 
The species has recently become more common in central and north Texas and is generally common in south Texas and south of the US border. It can also be found (nesting) in the Southern Caribbean (e.g. Aruba, Curaçao and Bonaire), Mexico, and Central America.


In Florida

Florida is home to a relict population of northern crested caracaras that dates to the last glacial period, which ended around 12,500 BP. At that time, Florida and the rest of the Gulf Coast were covered in an oak savanna. As temperatures increased, the savanna between Florida and Texas disappeared. Caracaras were able to survive in the prairies of central Florida and the marshes along the St. Johns River. Cabbage palmettos are a preferred nesting site, although they also nest in southern live oaks. Their historical range on the modern-day Florida peninsula included Okeechobee, Osceola, Highlands, Glades, Polk, Indian River, St. Lucie, Hardee, DeSoto, Brevard, Collier, and Martin counties. They are currently most common in DeSoto, Glades, Hendry, Highlands, Okeechobee, and Osceola Counties. It has been seen on the East Coast as far as extreme eastern Brevard County, Florida (Viera, Florida), where it is now considered a resident, but listed as threatened. In February 2023 a crested caracara was identified in St, Johns County, Florida and documented by The St. Johns County Audubon Society on their social media page.


Vagrancy
In July 2016 a northern caracara was reported and photographed by numerous people in the upper peninsula of Michigan, just outside of Munising. In June 2017, a northern caracara was sighted far north in St. George, New Brunswick, Canada. A specimen was photographed in Woodstock, Vermont in March 2020. 


Description
The crested caracara has a total length of 50–65 cm (20–26 in) and a wingspan of 120–132 cm (47–52 in). Its weight is 0.9–1.6 kg (2.0–3.5 lb), averaging 1,348 g (2.972 lb) in seven birds from Tierra del Fuego. Individuals from the colder southern part of its range average larger than those from tropical regions (as predicted by Bergmann's rule) and are the largest type of caracara. In fact, they are the second-largest species of falcon in the world by mean body mass, second only to the gyrfalcon. 
The cap, belly, thighs, most of the wings, and tail tip are dark brownish, the auriculars (feathers surrounding the ear), throat, and nape are whitish-buff, and the chest, neck, mantle, back, upper tail coverts, crissum (the undertail coverts surrounding the cloaca), and basal part of the tail are whitish-buff barred dark brownish. In flight, the outer primaries show a large conspicuous whitish-buff patch ('window'), as in several other species of caracaras. The legs are yellow and the bare facial skin and cere are deep yellow to reddish-orange. (The facial color can change depending on the bird's mood.) Juveniles resemble adults, but are paler, with streaking on the chest, neck, and back, grey legs, and whitish, later pinkish-purple, facial skin and cere.


Behavior
A bold, opportunistic raptor, the crested caracara is often seen walking around on the ground looking for food. It mainly feeds on carcasses of dead animals, but it also steals food from other raptors, raids bird and reptile nests, and takes live prey if the possibility arises; mostly this is insects or other small prey, such as small mammals, small birds, amphibians, reptiles, fish, crabs, other shellfish, maggots, and worms, but it can include creatures up to the size of a snowy egret. It may also eat fruit. It is dominant over the black and turkey vulture at carcasses. 
Furthermore, it also pirates food from them and buteos, as well as from brown pelicans, ibises, and spoonbills, chasing and harrying until they regurgitate or drop food. The crested caracara takes live prey that has been flushed by wildfire, cattle, and farming equipment. Locally, it has even learnt to
follow trains or cars for food thrown out. The opportunistic nature of this species means that the crested caracara seeks out the phenomena associated with its food, e.g. wildfires and circling vultures. It is typically solitary, but several individuals may gather at a large food source (e.g. dumps). Breeding takes place in the Southern Hemisphere spring/summer in the southern part of its range, but timing is less strict in warmer regions. The nest is a large, open structure, typically placed on the top of a tree or palm, but sometimes on the ground. The typical clutch size is two eggs.

There are several recorded instances of black vultures approaching and preening crested caracaras. In all cases, the unusual behaviour begins with the caracara bowing its head down, in an apparent invitation to preen.


Status

Throughout most of its range, its occurrence is common to very common. It is likely to benefit from the widespread deforestation in tropical South America, so is considered to be of least concern by BirdLife International.


Predation
In Florida, crested caracaras may be eaten by some growth stage of invasive snakes such as Burmese pythons, reticulated pythons, Southern African rock pythons, Central African rock pythons, boa constrictors, yellow anacondas, Bolivian anacondas, dark-spotted anacondas, and green anacondas.


In culture

Mexican ornithologist Rafael Martín del Campo proposed that the northern caracara was possibly the sacred "eagle" depicted in several pre-Columbian Aztec codices, as well as the Florentine Codex. This imagery was adopted as a national symbol of Mexico, but it is not the bird depicted on the flag, which is a golden eagle (Aquila chrysaetos), the national bird.


References


Further reading
Dove, C. & R. Banks. 1999. A Taxonomic study of Crested Caracaras (Falconidae). Wilson Bull. 111(3): 330–339. Available online (PDF) Archived 2012-03-02 at the Wayback Machine
Ferguson-Lees, J., D. Christie, P. Burton, K. Franklin & D. Mead (2001). Raptors of the World. Christopher Helm. ISBN 0-7136-8026-1
Restall, R., C. Rodner, & M. Lentino (2006). Birds of Northern South America. Vol. 1 & 2. Helm, London. ISBN 0-7136-7242-0 (vol. 1); ISBN 0-7136-7243-9 (vol. 2)
Schulenberg, T., D. Stotz, D. Lane, J. O'Neill, & T. Parker III (2007). Birds of Peru. Helm, London. ISBN 978-0-7136-8673-9
Meiburg, Jonathan (2022). "A Most Remarkable Creature: The Hidden Life of the World's Smartest Birds of Prey". Vintage, New York. ISBN 978-1101911549

The '''crested caracara''' ('''''Caracara plancus''''') is a [[bird of prey]] (raptor) in the [[falcon]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]], [[Falconidae]]. It was formerly placed in the [[genus]] ''Polyborus'' before being given in its own genus, ''[[Caracara (genus)|Caracara]]''. It is native to and found in the [[Southern United States|southern]] and [[southeastern United States]], [[Mexico]] (where it is present in every [[List of states of Mexico|state]]) and the majority of mainland [[Latin America]], as well as some [[List of Caribbean islands|Caribbean islands]]. The crested caracara is quite adaptable and hardy, for a species found predominantly in the neotropics; it can be found in a range of environments and ecosystems, including [[Semi-arid climate|semi-arid]] and [[desert]] climates, maritime or coastal areas, [[Subtropics|subtropical]] and [[Tropical forest|tropical forests]], [[Temperate climate|temperate]] regions, [[Plain|plains]], [[Swamp|swamps]], and even in urban areas. Documented, albeit rare, sightings have occurred as far north as [[Minnesota]] and the [[Provinces and territories of Canada|Canadian provinces]] of [[Alberta]], [[Ontario]], and [[Prince Edward Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Crested caracara (Caracara plancus) |first=Ontario sightings |date=2016 |title=Observations • iNaturalist |url=https://inaturalist.org/observations?place_id=6883&subview=map&taxon_id=4715}}</ref> The southern extent of the crested caracara's distribution can reach as far as [[Tierra del Fuego]] and [[Magallanes Region]], [[Chile]].
==Etymology==
The word "caracara" ultimately traces its origins to [[Old Tupi]] {{lang|tpn|karakará}}. Further etymology is uncertain, but it is assumed to be [[onomatopoeic]], imitating the bird's call.

The crested caracara is also known as the '''Mexican eagle''',<ref name="allaboutbirdsoverview">{{cite web |title=Crested Caracara Overview, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Crested_Caracara/overview |website=allaboutbirds.org |access-date=28 November 2023}}</ref> although it is not a true [[eagle]]. This stems from a 1960 proposal by {{interlanguage link|Rafael Martín del Campo|es}}, supposedly based on an analysis of pre-[[Spanish conquest of Mexico|Conquest]] imagery, that the "bird atop a [[Opuntia|prickly pear]]" associated with the mythical founding of [[Tenochtitlan]] was a caracara, and not a [[golden eagle]] (which is a rare sight in [[Mexico]], especially as far south as Tenochtitlan) as is traditionally depicted. Nonetheless, since at least the Spanish Conquest, the golden eagle has been the bird irrevocably associated with the founding myth, featuring prominently in independent Mexico's [[National Shield of Mexico|national insignia]] as well as being its national animal.

=== "Texan eagle" ===
[[Balduin Möllhausen]], the German artist accompanying the 1853 railroad survey (led by Lt. [[Amiel Weeks Whipple]]) from the [[Canadian River]] to California along the [[35th parallel north|35th parallel]], recounted observing what he called the "Texan Eagle", which, in his account, he identified as Audubon's ''Polyborus vulgaris''. This sighting occurred in the [[Sans Bois Mountains]] in southeastern Oklahoma.<ref name="Mollhausen">{{cite book |url=https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mollhausen_Diary_pp_44-45.png|last=Möllhausen |first=Balduin |year=1858 |title=Diary of a Journey from the Mississippi to Coasts of the Pacific With a United States Government Expedition |page=45 |publisher=Longman, Brown, Green, Longmans, & Roberts}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
In 1777, English illustrator [[John Frederick Miller]] included a hand-coloured plate of the crested caracara in his ''Icones animalium et plantarum'' ("icons of the animal and plant world"). He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Falco plancus'' and specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as [[Tierra del Fuego]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Miller | first=John Frederick | author-link=John Frederick Miller | year=1777 | title=Icones animalium et plantarum. Various subjects of Natural History, wherein are delineated Birds, Animals and many curious Plants, &c. | volume=1 |language=la | location=London | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49649848 | at=Part 3, Plate 17}} The work was published in 10 parts with 6 plates in each part. See: {{ cite journal | last1=Sherborn | first1=C.D. | author1-link=Charles Davies Sherborn | last2=Iredale | first2=T. | author2-link=Tom Iredale | date=1921 | title=J. F. Miller's ''Icones'' | journal=Ibis | volume=3 | series=11th series  | issue=2 | pages=302–309 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1921.tb00801.x | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/58569377 | ref=none}}</ref> The specific epithet ''plancus'' is [[Latin]] for "eagle".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=309 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n309/mode/1up }}</ref> The crested caracara is now placed in the genus ''[[Caracara (genus)|Caracara]]'' (which was introduced in 1826 by German naturalist [[Blasius Merrem]]).<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=Seriemas, falcons | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/falcons/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=3 October 2023 }}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''C. p. cheriway'' ([[Joseph Franz von Jacquin|Jacquin]], 1784) – [[United States]] ([[Southern California]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Observations • iNaturalist |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?place_id=14&subview=map&taxon_id=1290157 |access-date=2 April 2024 |website=[[iNaturalist]]}}</ref> [[Arizona]], [[Florida]], [[Louisiana]], [[New Mexico]], [[Texas]]),<ref>{{Cite web |title=Observations • iNaturalist |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?place_id=1&subview=map&taxon_id=1290157 |access-date=2 April 2024 |website=[[iNaturalist]]}}</ref> [[Mexico|México]] (present in every [[List of states of Mexico|state]]),<ref>{{Cite web |title=Observations • iNaturalist |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?place_id=6793&subview=map&taxon_id=4712 |access-date=2 April 2024 |website=[[iNaturalist]]}}</ref> [[Belize]], [[El Salvador]], [[Guatemala]], [[Honduras]], [[Nicaragua]], [[Costa Rica]], [[Panama|Panamá]], [[Colombia]], [[Venezuela]], [[Ecuador]], [[Guyana]], [[Suriname]], [[French Guiana]], and north [[Roraima|Roraima, Brazil]];<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Observations • iNaturalist |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?place_id=any&subview=map&taxon_id=1290157 |access-date=2 April 2024 |website=[[iNaturalist]]}}</ref> Caribbean islands of [[Cuba]], [[Aruba]], [[Guanaja]] and [[Roatán]] (Honduras), and [[Trinidad]]; the Pacific [[Islas Marías]] (Mexico) and [[Isla del Rey, Panama|Isla del Rey]] (Panamá).<ref name=":0" /> Individual birds have been seen as far north as [[Dallas|Dallas, Texas]] and [[Santa Cruz County, California|Santa Cruz, California]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Observations • iNaturalist |url=https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?place_id=418&subview=map&taxon_id=1290157 |access-date=2 April 2024 |website=[[iNaturalist]]}}</ref>
* ''C. p. plancus'' ([[John Frederick Miller|Miller, JF]], 1777) – SE [[Peru|Perú]], N [[Bolivia]] to [[Eastern Brazil marine ecoregion|Eastern Brazil]], south to [[Tierra del Fuego]] and the [[Falkland Islands]].

The subspecies ''C. p. cheriway'' was formerly classed as a separate species, with the common English name of the northern crested caracara.<ref name=ioc/><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Chesser | first1=R.T. | last2=Billerman | first2=S.M. | last3=Burns | first3=K.J. | last4=Cicero | first4=C. | last5=Dunn | first5=J.L. | last6=Hernández-Baños | first6=B.E. | last7=Kratter | first7=A.W. | last8=Lovette | first8=I.J. | last9=Mason | first9=N.A. | last10=Rasmussen | first10=P.C. | last11=Remsen | first11=J.V.J. | last12=Stotz | first12=D.F. | last13=Winker | first13=K. | date=2021 | title=Sixty-second Supplement to the American Ornithological Society's Check-list of North American Birds | journal=Ornithology | volume=138 | issue=ukab037 | doi=10.1093/ornithology/ukab037 | doi-access=free }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Crested Caracara, Ushuaia, Tierra del Fuego, Argentina imported from iNaturalist photo 266331930.jpg|desired_width=250|caption=Crested caracaras with a [[black-chested buzzard-eagle]], in Argentina|crop_right_perc=30|crop_top_perc=25}}{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Crested Caracara and American Black Vulture, Gallant Fox Road, Austin, Texas, USA 468881148.jpg|desired_width=250|caption=In conflict with a black vulture, in Texas|crop_left_perc=20|crop_right_perc=20|crop_top_perc=30|crop_bottom_perc=5}}
[[File:Caracara Courtship Display (44541482574).jpg|thumb|Courtship display, in Texas. The male, which is slightly smaller, is on the left.]]
The crested caracara occurs from [[Tierra del Fuego]] in southernmost South America to the southern United States, Mexico, and Central America. An isolated population occurs on the [[Falkland Islands]]. It avoids the [[Andean]] highlands and dense humid forests, such as the [[Amazon rainforest]], where it is largely restricted to relatively open sections along major rivers. Otherwise, it occurs in virtually any open or semi-open habitat and is often found near humans.{{Citation needed|date=January 2026}}

Reports have been made of the crested caracara as far north as [[San Francisco]], [[California]].<ref name="GGRO">{{cite web|title=Rare Raptors|url=http://www.ggro.org/rare-raptors.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091003132404/http://www.ggro.org/rare-raptors.html|archive-date=3 October 2009|access-date=22 August 2009|publisher=Golden Gate Raptor Observatory}}</ref> and, in 2012, near [[Crescent City, California]].<ref name="prj_noah">{{cite web|date=13 February 2012|title=caracara sighting record|url=http://www.projectnoah.org/spottings/8974007|website=Project Noah}}</ref> Some are believed to possibly be living in [[Nova Scotia]], with numerous sightings throughout the 2010s.<ref>{{Cite news|title=What's this crested caracara doing in Nova Scotia? {{!}} CBC News|language=en-US|work=CBC|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/nova-scotia/birds-nova-scotia-david-currie-1.3273239|access-date=2021-01-14}}</ref> 

The species has recently become more common in central and north Texas and is generally common in south Texas and south of the US border.{{Citation needed|date=April 2020}} It can also be found (nesting) in the Southern Caribbean (e.g. [[Aruba]], [[Curaçao]] and [[Bonaire]]){{Citation needed|date=April 2020}}, [[Mexico]], and [[Central America]].

===In Florida===
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Crested Caracara, John Paul Landing Park imported from iNaturalist photo 279587601.jpg|desired_width=250|align=right|caption=Showing a full [[crop (anatomy)|crop]], in Texas|crop_top_perc=10|crop_bottom_perc=25|crop_left_perc=20|crop_right_perc=20}}
Florida is home to a [[glacial relict|relict population]] of northern crested caracaras that dates to the [[last glacial period]], which ended around 12,500 [[Before Present|BP]]. At that time, Florida and the rest of the [[Gulf Coast of the United States|Gulf Coast]] were covered in an [[oak savanna]]. As temperatures increased, the savanna between Florida and Texas disappeared.<ref name="relict">{{cite web |url=http://www.indianriverlagoonenvirothon.com/wildlife_resource/Chapter%20VIII.htm |title=Chapter VIII. Florida Relict Species |work=Resource Guide |publisher=Indian River Lagoon Envirothon |access-date=23 January 2009}}{{Dead link|date=August 2014}}</ref> Caracaras were able to survive in the [[prairie]]s of central Florida and the [[marsh]]es along the [[St. Johns River]]. [[Sabal palmetto|Cabbage palmettos]] are a preferred nesting site, although they also nest in [[Quercus virginiana|southern live oaks]].<ref name="FWS">{{cite web |url=http://www.fws.gov/verobeach/MSRPPDFs/AudubonsCrestedCaracara.pdf |title=Audubon's Crested Caracara |work=South Florida Ecological Services Office |publisher=[[United States Fish and Wildlife Service]] |access-date=23 January 2009 |archive-date=5 February 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210205174850/https://www.fws.gov/verobeach/MSRPPDFs/AudubonsCrestedCaracara.pdf }}</ref> Their historical range on the modern-day Florida peninsula included [[Okeechobee County, Florida|Okeechobee]], [[Osceola County, Florida|Osceola]], [[Highlands County, Florida|Highlands]], [[Glades County, Florida|Glades]], [[Polk County, Florida|Polk]], [[Indian River County, Florida|Indian River]], [[St. Lucie County, Florida|St. Lucie]], [[Hardee County, Florida|Hardee]], [[DeSoto County, Florida|DeSoto]], [[Brevard County, Florida|Brevard]], [[Collier County, Florida|Collier]], and [[Martin County, Florida|Martin]] counties.<ref name="Archbold">{{cite conference|last1=Morrison|first1=J.L.|date=October 2004|editor-last=Noss|editor-first=R.|title=The Crested Caracara in the changing grasslands of Florida|url=http://www.ces.fau.edu/fdpc/proceedings/3-17145_p.21115_Mor_FDPC_d.pdf|conference=Proceedings of the Florida Dry Prairie Conference, October 2004|location=Sebring, Florida|pages=211–215|book-title=Land of Fire and Water: The Florida Dry Prairie Ecosystem}}</ref> They are currently most common in DeSoto, Glades, Hendry, Highlands, Okeechobee, and Osceola Counties.<ref name="FFWCC">{{cite web |url=https://myfwc.com/wildlifehabitats/profiles/birds/raptors-and-vultures/crested-caracara/ |title=Species Profile: Crested Caracara |publisher=Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission |access-date=20 March 2024 }}</ref> It has been seen on the East Coast as far as extreme eastern [[Brevard County, Florida]] ([[Viera, Florida]]), where it is now considered a resident, but listed as [[Endangered Species Act|threatened]]. In February 2023 a crested caracara was identified in St, Johns County, Florida and documented by The St. Johns County Audubon Society on their social media page.

=== Vagrancy ===
In July 2016 a northern caracara was reported and photographed by numerous people in the upper peninsula of Michigan, just outside of [[Munising, Michigan|Munising]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Scot|first1=Stewart|title=News|url=https://www.sundewphotography.com/news.html|access-date=August 2, 2016|website=Nature Photography by Scot Stewart}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=Bernard|first1=Daryl|title=Crested Caracara|url=https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/3669221|access-date=August 2, 2016|website=iNaturalist.org|archive-date=July 14, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190714130839/https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/3669221}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore Instagram|url=https://www.instagram.com/p/BHsxdnZB3aw/|url-access=limited|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/iarchive/s/instagram/BHsxdnZB3aw|archive-date=2021-12-24|access-date=August 2, 2016}}{{cbignore}}</ref> In June 2017, a northern caracara was sighted far north in [[St. George, New Brunswick|St. George]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Canada]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Corbett|first1=Tanya|title=News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/new-brunswick/nb-rare-bird-nb-1.4181506|access-date=June 28, 2017|website=CBC News}}</ref> A specimen was photographed in [[Woodstock, Vermont]] in March 2020.{{Citation needed|date=April 2020}} 

==Description==
The crested caracara has a total length of {{cvt|50|–|65|cm|in||abbr=on}} and a wingspan of {{cvt|120|–|132|cm|in|abbr=on}}. Its weight is {{cvt|0.9|–|1.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, averaging {{convert|1348|g|lb|abbr=on}} in seven birds from [[Tierra del Fuego]].<ref>[http://www.zootierliste.de/en/?klasse=2&ordnung=208&familie=20803&art=21103062 Info about the southern caracara] at Zootierliste.de</ref><ref name="CRC">''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses, 2nd Edition'' by John B. Dunning Jr. (Editor). CRC Press (2008), {{ISBN|978-1-4200-6444-5}}.</ref> Individuals from the colder southern part of its range average larger than those from [[tropical]] regions (as predicted by [[Bergmann's rule]]) and are the largest type of caracara. In fact, they are the second-largest species of falcon in the world by mean body mass, second only to the [[gyrfalcon]].<ref name="CRC" /> 

The cap, belly, thighs, most of the wings, and tail tip are dark brownish, the auriculars (feathers surrounding the ear), throat, and nape are whitish-buff, and the chest, neck, [[Glossary of bird terms#mantle|mantle]], back, upper tail [[Covert (feather)|coverts]], [[crissum]] (the undertail coverts surrounding the [[cloaca]]), and [[basal (anatomy)|basal]] part of the tail are whitish-buff barred dark brownish. In flight, the outer [[Flight feather#Primaries|primaries]] show a large conspicuous whitish-buff patch ('window'), as in several other species of [[Caracara (subfamily)|caracara]]s. The legs are yellow and the bare facial skin and [[cere]] are deep yellow to reddish-orange. (The facial color can change depending on the bird's mood.)<ref name="peregrinefund.org" /> Juveniles resemble adults, but are paler, with streaking on the chest, neck, and back, grey legs, and whitish, later pinkish-purple, facial skin and cere.

==Behavior==
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Crested Caracara, Santa Fe de la Vera Cruz, Santa Fe, Argentina imported from iNaturalist photo 297801652.jpg|desired_width=250|align=right|caption=Catching a [[feral pigeon]], in Argentina|crop_left_perc=35}}A bold, opportunistic [[Bird of prey|raptor]], the crested caracara is often seen walking around on the ground looking for food. It mainly feeds on carcasses of dead animals, but it also steals food from other raptors, raids [[bird]] and [[reptile]]<ref name="animaldiversity.org">{{cite web | url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Caracara_cheriway/ | title=Caracara cheriway (Crested caracara) | website=[[Animal Diversity Web]] }}</ref> nests, and takes live prey if the possibility arises; mostly this is insects or other small prey, such as small [[mammal]]s, small birds, [[amphibian]]s, reptiles, [[fish]], [[crab]]s, other [[shellfish]], [[maggot]]s, and [[worm]]s,<ref name="peregrinefund.org">{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/falcons/crested-caracara | title=Crested Caracara | the Peregrine Fund }}</ref><ref name="animaldiversity.org" /><ref name="Ferguson-Lees, J. 2001">Ferguson-Lees, J. & Christie, D.A. & Franklin, K. & Mead, D. & Burton, P.. (2001). Raptors of the world. Helm Identification Guides.</ref> but it can include creatures up to the size of a [[snowy egret]]. It may also eat fruit. It is dominant over the [[American black vulture|black]] and [[turkey vulture]] at carcasses. 
Furthermore, it also pirates food from them and [[buteo]]s, as well as from [[brown pelican]]s, [[ibis]]es, and [[spoonbill]]s, chasing and harrying until they regurgitate or drop food.<ref name="Ferguson-Lees, J. 2001" /> The crested caracara takes live prey that has been flushed by wildfire, cattle, and farming equipment. Locally, it has even learnt to
follow trains or cars for food thrown out.<ref name="Ferguson-Lees, J. 2001" /> The opportunistic nature of this species means that the crested caracara seeks out the phenomena associated with its food, e.g. wildfires and circling vultures.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Dunne |first1=Pete |title=Hawks in flight: the flight identification of North American raptors |date=2012 |publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |location=Boston |isbn=978-0-395--70959-7 |page=226 |edition=2nd}}</ref> It is typically solitary, but several individuals may gather at a large food source (e.g. dumps). Breeding takes place in the Southern Hemisphere spring/summer in the southern part of its range, but timing is less strict in warmer regions. The nest is a large, open structure, typically placed on the top of a tree or [[Arecaceae|palm]], but sometimes on the ground. The typical clutch size is two eggs.{{Citation needed|date=January 2026}}
<gallery mode="packed">
File:Caracara Plancus (Carancho) 2.jpg|Young adult, showing the distinctive light 'windows' in the wings
File:Crested caracara (Caracara plancus plancus) in flight Leona Amarga.jpg|adult, showing wing upperside
File:Carcara.jpg|Being [[Mobbing behavior|mobbed]] by a [[fork-tailed flycatcher]]
File:Crested caracaras (Caracara plancus plancus) on carcass Torres del Paine.jpg|At a sheep carcass
File:Caracará (Caracara plancus).JPG|Walking along a beach, in [[Ilhabela]], Brazil
</gallery>
[[File:Black vulture and crested caracara (1).jpg|thumb|[[Black vulture]] preening a crested caracara, in Texas]]
There are several recorded instances of [[Black vulture|black vultures]] approaching and [[Preening#Allopreening|preening]] crested caracaras. In all cases, the unusual behaviour begins with the caracara bowing its head down, in an apparent invitation to preen.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boies |first=Lori |last2=Tinacba |first2=Franchesca |last3=Shackleford |first3=Terry J. |date=2026-01-12 |title=Urban Green Spaces Set the Stage for Rare Interspecific Allopreening Between Crested Caracara (  Caracara plancus  ) and Black Vultures (  Coragyps atratus  ) |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/ece3.72669 |journal=Ecology and Evolution |language=en |volume=16 |issue=1 |doi=10.1002/ece3.72669 |issn=2045-7758 |pmc=12794025 |pmid=41531915}}</ref>

==Status==
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Crested Caracara eating a turtle (16935208046).jpg|desired_width=250|align=right|caption=Feeding on a [[terrapin]], in Texas|crop_bottom_perc=25|crop_right_perc=10}}
Throughout most of its range, its occurrence is common to very common. It is likely to benefit from the widespread [[deforestation]] in tropical South America, so is considered to be of [[least concern]] by [[BirdLife International]].

==Predation==
In Florida, crested caracaras may be eaten by some growth stage of invasive snakes such as [[Burmese python]]s, [[reticulated python]]s, [[Southern African rock python]]s, [[Central African rock python]]s, [[boa constrictor]]s, [[yellow anaconda]]s, [[Bolivian anaconda]]s, [[dark-spotted anaconda]]s, and [[green anaconda]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Final environmental assessment for the large constrictor snakes listed as injurious wildlife under the Lacey Act|date=January 2012|publisher=U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service|url=https://downloads.regulations.gov/FWS-R9-FHC-2008-0015-4565-content.pdf}}</ref>

== In culture ==
{{Multiple image
| image1            = Crested Caracara 2 (cropped).jpg
| total_width       = 400
| image2            = Crested Caracara (34922405766) (cropped).jpg
| footer            = Adult (left) and juvenile (right), in Texas
}}
Mexican [[ornithology|ornithologist]] Rafael Martín del Campo proposed that the northern caracara was possibly the sacred "eagle" depicted in several [[pre-Columbian]] [[Aztec codices]], as well as the [[Florentine Codex]]. This imagery was adopted as a [[Coat of arms of Mexico|national symbol]] of [[Mexico]], but it is not the bird depicted on the [[Flag of Mexico|flag]], which is a [[golden eagle]] (''Aquila chrysaetos''), the [[List of national birds|national bird]].<ref name="Gonzalez2004">{{cite journal |last=González Block |first=Miguel A. |title=El Iztaccuahtli y el Águila Mexicana: ¿Cuauhtli o Águila Real? |year=2004 |journal=[[Arqueología Mexicana]] |volume=XII |issue=70 |pages=60–65 |language=es |url=http://www.arqueomex.com/S2N3nAGUILA70.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090216140206/http://arqueomex.com/S2N3nAGUILA70.html |archive-date=2009-02-16 }}</ref>

[[zh:鳳頭卡拉鷹]]

zh.extract:
巨隼属（學名：Caracara，台湾作卡拉卡拉隼屬），是隼科巨隼亞科下的一個屬，其下現存凤头巨隼一個種，並有北美及南美兩種亞種，這兩個亞種過去被認為是兩個物種但現被合併。此外還有許多化石種存在，而北美凤头卡拉隼的化石也曾发现于拉布雷亚沥青坑中。


分類

凤头巨隼 Caracara plancus (Miller, JF, 1777)
†瓜达卢长腿兀鹰 Caracara lutosa (Ridgway, 1876)


史前生物
根據化石記錄，該屬曾擁有以下幾個種：

†Caracara creightoni：更新世晚期
†Caracara tellustris：更新世晚期
†Caracara major：更新世晚期
†Caracara latebrosus：更新世晚期
†Caracara seymouri：更新世晚期


参考文献


扩展阅读
Dove, C. & R. Banks. 1999. A Taxonomic study of Crested Caracaras (Falconidae). （页面存档备份，存于互联网档案馆） Wilson Bull. 111(3): 330–339.
